message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 7
Recent Green phase: No code change — 2-neighbor survival test already satisfied.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
PRE measurements established. Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe per function.

## Step 0: PRE-Measurements (ESLint)

- **ESLint smells**: 0 violations
- **Cognitive complexity**: Not flagged (all below threshold)

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

Walking through each function:

- `cellKey([x, y])`: Returns a string key for a cell. Name fits.
- `parseCellKey(k)`: Inverse — parses string back to Cell. Name fits.
- `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS`: Constant of 8 (dx, dy) pairs. Name fits.
- `countLiveNeighbors(cells)`: Tallies how many live cells each neighbor position has. Name fits — it counts how many live neighbors each position has.
- `isAliveNext(neighborCount, isAlive)`: Returns whether the cell will be alive next generation. Name fits.
- `nextGeneration(cells)`: Top-level entry point. Name fits.

All names accurately reveal intent. **No renames needed.**

## Step 2: PRE-Measurement (APP Mass)

**`cellKey`**:
- Bindings: 3 (x, y, parameter destructure)
- Invocations: 1 (template literal `${x},${y}` ~ string concat)
- Mass: 3·1 + 1·2 = **5**

**`parseCellKey`**:
- Constants: 1 (",")
- Bindings: 3 (k, xs, ys)
- Invocations: 3 (split, Number×2)
- Mass: 1·1 + 3·1 + 3·2 = **10**

**`NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS`** (data, not function): 16 constants = 16

**`countLiveNeighbors`**:
- Constants: 1 (0 default)
- Bindings: 6 (cells, counts, x, y, dx, dy, k) ~ 7
- Invocations: 5 (new Map, cellKey, set, get, +)
- Loops: 2
- Mass: 1·1 + 7·1 + 5·2 + 0·4 + 2·5 + 0·6 = **28**

**`isAliveNext`**:
- Bindings: 2 (neighborCount, isAlive)
- Invocations: 3 (=== ×2, &&, ||) — count as comparison ops
- Conditionals: 0 (just boolean expression returned)
- Mass: 2·1 + 3·2 = **8**

**`nextGeneration`**:
- Bindings: 5 (cells, alive, neighborCounts, next, k, count) ~ 6
- Invocations: 5 (new Set, map, cellKey, countLiveNeighbors, has, isAliveNext, push, parseCellKey) ~ 8
- Conditionals: 1 (if)
- Loops: 1 (for-of)
- Mass: 6·1 + 8·2 + 1·4 + 1·5 = **31**

## Step 3: PRE-Measurement (McCabe)

- `cellKey`: 1 (no branches)
- `parseCellKey`: 1 (no branches)
- `countLiveNeighbors`: 1 + 2 loops + 1 (`??`) = **4**
- `isAliveNext`: 1 + `||` + `&&` = **3**
- `nextGeneration`: 1 + 1 loop + 1 if = **3**

**Worst McCabe**: `countLiveNeighbors` at 4. **Worst APP mass**: `nextGeneration` at 31.

## Refactoring Analysis

Looking at the code carefully, I see a real opportunity: `nextGeneration` does work that could be simplified. Currently:

1. Builds `alive` Set from `cells.map(cellKey)`
2. Builds `neighborCounts` Map (separate pass over cells)
3. Iterates the neighbor count map and checks `alive.has(k)` per entry

The `cellKey`/`parseCellKey` round-trip is wasteful (serialize → parse). Also note that `countLiveNeighbors` excludes the cell itself from its own neighbor count (by design — `[0,0]` is not in `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS`), which is correct.

Looking at potential refactorings:

**Option A**: Could we eliminate the `parseCellKey` by storing the original `[x,y]` alongside the count? Yes — we could store `Map<string, { cell: Cell, count: number }>`. This eliminates `parseCellKey` entirely and saves the string-parse round-trip.

Let me explore Option A which would eliminate one function entirely and the string parsing overhead.

**Predicted post-refactor metrics for Option A**:
- Remove `parseCellKey` entirely (mass 10, McCabe 1 gone)
- `countLiveNeighbors` slightly heavier (stores object)
- `nextGeneration` lighter (no parse call)

Let me check though — is there a simpler win? Looking at `isAliveNext`:
```ts
neighborCount === NEIGHBORS_TO_BIRTH ||
(neighborCount === NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE && isAlive)
```

This is already very clean — Conway's rule expressed as one boolean expression. McCabe 3 but reads naturally.

Let me pursue Option A — it eliminates an entire helper function and the parse step. Let me apply it.

content.text:
Tests still pass. Now let me re-measure POST.

(The TaskCreate/Update reminder doesn't apply — this is a single short refactoring pass, not multi-task work.)

## Step 5: POST-Measurement (ESLint)

- **ESLint smells**: 0 (still clean)
- **Cognitive complexity**: still below threshold

## Step 6: POST-Measurement (APP Mass + McCabe)

**`cellKey`** (unchanged):
- Mass: **5**, McCabe: **1**

**`parseCellKey`**: **ELIMINATED** (was mass 10, McCabe 1)

**`tallyNeighbors`** (was `countLiveNeighbors`):
- Constants: 1 (`1` literal × 2 = 2)
- Bindings: 8 (cells, tallies, x, y, dx, dy, neighbor, k, existing)
- Invocations: 5 (new Map, cellKey, get, set, += internal ops)
- Conditionals: 1 (if/else)
- Loops: 2
- Assignments: 1 (existing.count += 1)
- Mass: 2·1 + 8·1 + 5·2 + 1·4 + 2·5 + 1·6 = **40** (up from 28)
- McCabe: 1 + 2 loops + 1 if = **4** (unchanged)

**`isAliveNext`** (unchanged):
- Mass: **8**, McCabe: **3**

**`nextGeneration`**:
- Bindings: 6 (cells, alive, tallies, next, k, cell, count) ~ 7
- Invocations: 7 (new Set, map, cellKey, tallyNeighbors, has, isAliveNext, push) — removed `parseCellKey`
- Conditionals: 1 (if)
- Loops: 1
- Mass: 7·1 + 7·2 + 1·4 + 1·5 = **30** (down from 31)
- McCabe: 1 + 1 loop + 1 if = **3** (unchanged)

## Aggregate Comparison

| Metric | PRE | POST | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| ESLint smells | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Total functions | 5 helpers + `nextGeneration` | 4 helpers + `nextGeneration` | -1 function |
| Total mass (sum) | 5+10+28+8+31 = **82** | 5+40+8+30 = **83** | **+1** |
| Max McCabe | 4 (`countLiveNeighbors`) | 4 (`tallyNeighbors`) | 0 |
| Tests | 7/7 passing | 7/7 passing | green |

The aggregate mass went **up by 1** (from 82 → 83). The `tallyNeighbors` function got heavier (28 → 40) because storing the object structure and the if/else branch added an assignment (mass 6) and a conditional (mass 4) without removing the loop or invocations. The savings in `nextGeneration` (−1) and elimination of `parseCellKey` (−10) didn't compensate.

This is a **measured regression in aggregate mass**. Per the workflow rule "If a measurement got worse, revert the change and try a different angle."

Let me revert.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_16-52-13_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
Reverted. Now let me try an alternative angle. Looking at the worst-mass function `nextGeneration` (mass 31), can I extract its for-loop with conditional into a more compact form?

**Alternative B**: Replace the imperative for/if/push with a `filter`+`map` pipeline. This trades the for-loop and if (loop=5, conditional=4) for a filter call (loop=5, mass-wise equivalent to one invocation but folded into a chain).

Let me compute predicted mass for Option B:

```ts
export function nextGeneration(cells: Cell[]): Cell[] {
  const alive = new Set(cells.map(cellKey));
  const neighborCounts = countLiveNeighbors(cells);
  return Array.from(neighborCounts)
    .filter(([k, count]) => isAliveNext(count, alive.has(k)))
    .map(([k]) => parseCellKey(k));
}
```

Component count:
- Bindings: cells, alive, neighborCounts, k, count, k = 6
- Invocations: new Set, map, cellKey, countLiveNeighbors, Array.from, filter, has, isAliveNext, map, parseCellKey = 10
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 3 (map, filter, map) — by APP definition each `map`/`filter` is a loop
- Mass: 6·1 + 10·2 + 0·4 + 3·5 = **41** — worse than 31.

That's worse. Filter+map adds invocation cost and extra loop instances.

**Alternative C**: Could I inline `parseCellKey` into the conditional branch? That just moves mass around. Mass stays similar.

**Alternative D**: The `cellKey` round-trip is genuinely wasteful but the alternatives we've explored all cost more in APP-mass terms. The current code is well-balanced: each helper has minimal mass, the top-level orchestration is clear.

**Alternative E**: Look at the `??` operator in `countLiveNeighbors` — `counts.get(k) ?? 0`. Could be slightly clearer but McCabe counts `??` as +1 and removing it would require an `if`/`else` (+1 conditional, +1 assignment) — net worse.

**Alternative F**: Naming — could `cellKey` be `serializeCell` and `parseCellKey` be `deserializeCell`? Slightly more symmetrical, but `cellKey`/`parseCellKey` already convey the key/parse intent clearly. The current names mention "key" which signals "for use as Map key" — that's more informative than the generic "serialize". **No rename.**

Let me verify the reverted state passes tests.

content.text:
Reverted state is green (7/7 tests pass, 0 ESLint smells). 

## Final Report

**Refactoring Attempt 1 (Reverted)**: Eliminated `parseCellKey` by storing original `Cell` in a `NeighborTally` object alongside the count.

Pre/Post deltas measured:
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0 (unchanged)
- Aggregate APP mass: 82 → 83 (**+1, worse**)
  - `tallyNeighbors`: 28 → 40 (extra assignment + conditional from if/else branch outweighed elimination of `parseCellKey`)
  - `nextGeneration`: 31 → 30 (saved one invocation)
  - `parseCellKey`: 10 → 0 (eliminated)
- Max McCabe: 4 → 4 (unchanged)

The measurement showed the refactoring traded a clean one-line `set(k, get(k) ?? 0 + 1)` for an `if/else` branch with an assignment — a worse APP profile because assignments cost 6 each. Reverted per workflow rule.

**Refactoring Attempts 2–6 (Rejected at prediction stage)**:
- Filter/map pipeline in `nextGeneration`: predicted mass 31 → 41 (worse, more loops + invocations)
- Inlining `parseCellKey`: zero net mass change, hurts clarity (Rule 2)
- Removing `??` in `countLiveNeighbors`: would force if/else, net worse
- Renaming `cellKey`/`parseCellKey` to serialize/deserialize: less informative ("key" signals Map key purpose)

**Final Pre/Post Deltas (no change)**:
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| ESLint smells | 0 (unchanged) |
| Cognitive complexity | below threshold (unchanged) |
| Max McCabe | 4 in `countLiveNeighbors` (unchanged) |
| Aggregate APP mass | 82 (unchanged) |
| Tests | 7/7 passing |

The current implementation has each helper at or near its mass floor for the behavior it expresses: `cellKey` (5), `parseCellKey` (10), `isAliveNext` (8), `countLiveNeighbors` (28), `nextGeneration` (31). Every structural alternative measured produced a worse aggregate.

Refactoring attempted, measured, reverted based on data. No net change applied.

**Relevant file**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_16-52-13_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`
